Columnist

OPINION | ROBERT STEINBUCH: March Madness

11 hours, 55 minutes ago by Robert Steinbuch

Enter your email below to follow similar stories

Success!


If you're like most Arkansans, you envision elections coinciding with crisp November days, yard signs sprouting like weeds, and that iconic if not slightly virtue-signaling "I Voted" sticker. But in the Natural State, the real political fireworks happen in March, not November.

I'm not talking about bracket-busting basketball upsets. I'm referring to our primary and judicial elections, which fly under the radar for far too many voters.

This year, Arkansas holds its primary elections--the contests where the political parties pick their nominees--on March 3, with early voting kicking off on February 16.

Because March isn't "election season" in the American psyche, however, voter turnout is low. That notwithstanding, in many Arkansas districts--especially rural ones--the GOP nominee is basically a shoo-in for the general election. Democrats might not even field a candidate. If they do, the effort is often symbolic.

Out of 135 seats in the Arkansas General Assembly, Republicans hold 82 in the House and 29 in the Senate as of recently. In the safely red districts, the real fight happens intra-party. Win the Republican primary, and November is just a victory lap. So for a state as red as ours, the primary election is often the main event.

Then there are the judicial elections. Unlike federal judges, who are appointed, Arkansans vote on our robed class. This occurs only during our March elections. These are important positions--folks who interpret laws on everything from criminal justice to family disputes.

This year, the March election includes two openings on the Supreme Court--plus intermediate-appellate and general-trial (called "circuit") judgeships. These races are nonpartisan, meaning no R or D appears next to names, which sounds fair, but often leads to low-information voting.

Two positions--three and six--on the Supreme Court are open this election. Three candidates are running. Each candidate must indicate for which seat he's running. No candidate may run for more than one seat.

So this year, only one seat is contested, because two of the candidates chose to run for position three. And the third candidate is running for position six, unopposed.

There's a bit more to how each Supreme Court candidate chose which seat to run for. Two of the candidates had no choice. They aren't permitted to run for the alternative positions as a function of law, because they were previously appointed to those spots. So really only one of the three candidates made a conscious decision as to which position to seek.

To make matters even more confusing, while elections for the Supreme Court are statewide, that's not the case for the Court of Appeals. For the state's intermediate appellate court--which handles most appeals from trial courts, and has statewide jurisdiction, just like the Supreme Court--its 12 judges are elected from seven appellate districts that divide the state geographically.

Most districts elect two judges. Two districts only elect one judge.

The Court of Appeals was established in 1978 to alleviate the caseload of the Supreme Court. Cases typically are assigned randomly to three-judge panels, not to all the judges at once--how the Supreme Court operates (with its seven justices).

The structure for choosing Court of Appeals judges seeks to promote geographic diversity. (I think I prefer the Supreme Court's election process, but haven't thought much about it.)

Here are my picks (so far) for several contested state races in which I know enough to have a preference. (I'm not covering federal offices here.)

Republican Primary:

Cole Jester (Land Commissioner)

Ron Caldwell (state Senate)

John Kyle Day (state representative) You'll recall my columns about Day's opponent, Missy Wardlaw, who has guilty dispositions for the crimes of: 1. issuing a hot check and 2. contempt of court because she skipped out on a trial date for a traffic charge. But don't let my belief that Wardlaw is unqualified lead you to conclude that my support for Day merely is by default. Oh, no. Day is remarkably capable and intelligent. His district is lucky to have such an outstanding candidate.

Wayne Long (state representative)

Richard Friend (Saline County sheriff)

Judges:

Nick Bronni (Supreme Court justice, position three) Conservative Cody Hiland is running for position six. While he has my complete support, the outcome of his race is already determined; he has no opponent.

Brandon Carter (Court of Appeals judge, district three)

Seth Creed (Circuit Court judge, Washington/Madison counties)

Scott Davidson (Circuit Court judge, Independence/Cleburne/Izard/Fulton/Stone counties)

Gunner DeLay (Circuit Court judge, Sebastian County)

Robert Tellez (Circuit Court judge, Pulaski County) I've known Tellez since he was my student many, many years ago. As a resident of Pulaski County, I'll be voting for him. He's great!

So don't skip the March elections. Showing up means claiming your say in the races that often decide everything.

This is your right to know.

Robert Steinbuch, the Arkansas Bar Foundation Professor at the Bowen Law School, is a Fulbright Scholar and author of the treatise "The Arkansas Freedom of Information Act." His views do not necessarily reflect those of his employer.


Support journalism that digs deeper into topics that matter most to Arkansans. Donate today to preserve the quality and integrity of local journalism.

Robert Steinbuch
